items.title: Understanding Tularemia Transmission and Pathogenicity
items.content: Tularemia is a zoonotic disease transmitted from vertebrate animals to humans, causing febrile illness. It's caused by the bacterium Francisella tularensis, which is highly virulent for both humans and numerous mammals.
keyPoints: Francisella tularensis is a small (0.2–0.7 × 0.2 μm), facultatively intracellular, aerobic, gram-negative, pleomorphic bacterium.
Highly virulent for humans and various mammals including rodents, hares, and rabbits.
Laboratory work must be performed under biosafety level 3 conditions due to its potential for airborne laboratory-acquired infection.

items.title: Distinguishing Subspecies of Francisella tularensis and Their Clinical Relevance
items.content: There are two subspecies of F. tularensis with significant clinical importance: subspecies tularensis (type A) and subspecies holarctica (type B). Type A is more lethal in humans, particularly documented in the United States.
keyPoints: Subspecies tularensis (type A) has human disease documentation exclusively from North America.
Subspecies holarctica (type B) has been documented globally.
Type A strains are significantly more lethal than type B strains in humans, with higher mortality rates observed in the United States.

items.title: Modes of Transmission and Geographic Distribution
items.content: F. tularensis infects humans through various routes including arthropod bites, inhalation of aerosols, handling infected animals, or ingestion of contaminated water. The disease is endemic to the Northern Hemisphere with a patchy distribution due to its maintenance in smaller geographic areas.
keyPoints: Transmission: arthropods (e.g., mosquitoes, ticks), inhalation, animal contact, contaminated water
Geographic distribution: endemic in Northern Hemisphere, unevenly distributed
US cases: 200–300 annually from 2010-2020, mainly tick bites in Arkansas, Kansas, Missouri, Oklahoma
Europe/Asia type B prevalence: several hundred to >2000 cases in peak years (Finland, Sweden, Turkey)
Seasonal patterns: summer in US, Finland, Sweden; winter in Turkey due to water contamination

items.title: Disease Ecology and Host Associations
items.content: Distinct disease ecologies characterize F. tularensis subspecies tularensis (type A) and holarctica (type B). Type A is associated with rabbits, ticks, domestic cats, and sheep in dry environments, while type B is linked to semiaquatic animals and water bodies.
keyPoints: Type A ecology: rabbits, ticks, domestic cats, sheep, dry environments
Type B ecology: streams, ponds, lakes, rivers, muskrats, beavers

items.title: Maintenance and Persistence of F. tularensis in Nature
items.content: The maintenance mechanisms of F. tularensis are not fully understood. It is a generalist microbe infecting many animals, with potential persistence through decaying tissues or protozoa like amoeba.
keyPoints: Generalist host: infects amphibians, birds, rodents, rabbits, carnivores, ruminants
Maintenance hypotheses: decaying infectious tissues, persistence in water with protozoa

items.title: Tularemia Transmission Dynamics in Ticks
items.content: F. tularensis is efficiently transmitted by adult ticks, which acquire the bacterium from infected hosts and transmit it with high efficiency to new hosts. However, immature nymphs are ineffective vectors due to low survival and transmission rates.
keyPoints: Adult ticks show 58–89% transmission efficiency of F. tularensis to uninfected mice.
Immature nymphs have a transmission efficiency of 0–13.5%, indicating ineffectiveness as disease vectors.
Ticks are important for transmitting tularemia during epizootics but may not be essential for maintaining the bacterium between outbreaks.

items.title: Pathogenesis of F. tularensis Infection in Humans
items.content: F. tularensis can cause disease through skin microtrauma or inhalation, requiring only a low dose of 10–50 bacteria. It manipulates host-cell signaling to replicate within macrophages and disseminates systemically.
keyPoints: Infection requires an extremely low dose (10–50) when inoculated through the skin or inhaled.
F. tularensis is a 'stealth pathogen' that manipulates host-cell signaling for replication within macrophages.
Oral infection requires a much higher dose ($ 10^{6} $– $ 10^{8} $) to establish infection.

items.title: Immune Response and Pathology in F. tularensis Infection
items.content: F. tularensis delays immune responses, allowing rapid systemic spread. Effective immune responses involve complement, antibodies, neutrophils, and cytokines like IFN-γ, TNF-α, and IL-12.
keyPoints: Cell-mediated responses require both B and T cells, with antigen-specific recall T cell responses persisting 25–30 years post-infection or vaccination.
Pathology includes necrotic lesions in spleen by day 4 post-inhalation infection, progressing to larger foci in liver and lymph nodes.
Granuloma formation and necrotic lesions surrounded by macrophages, lymphocytes, and giant cells are typical from day 7 onward.

items.title: Recognizing Tularemia to Prevent Misdiagnosis
items.content: Tularemia can be challenging to diagnose due to its nonspecific symptoms, which may mimic other conditions such as lung cancer or tuberculosis. Clinicians should maintain a high index of suspicion and consider tularemia in patients with acute fever onset and influenza-like illness, especially if there is potential exposure to F. tularensis.
keyPoints: Tularemia symptoms can be mistaken for other conditions like lung cancer, lymphoma, or tuberculosis.
A high index of suspicion is critical for early recognition.
Key history includes acute fever onset with influenza-like illness and potential exposure to F. tularensis.

items.title: Assessing Severity and Determining Care Level in Tularemia
items.content: Physical examination should focus on identifying the anatomic location and extent of inflammation, such as pneumonitis or lymph node involvement. Assess the rate of progression and severity to determine if outpatient care is sufficient or if inpatient care is needed due to multiorgan involvement or hemodynamic instability.
keyPoints: Examine for signs of pneumonitis, lymph node inflammation, skin changes, and pharyngitis.
Evaluate the rate of progression and infection severity.
Decide on outpatient vs. inpatient care based on symptom localization and severity.

items.title: Recognizing Tularemia Forms Based on Infection Route
items.content: Tularemia clinical manifestations vary based on the route of infection. Skin or oral mucous membrane infections lead to regional lymph node enlargement, while inhalation results in mediastinal lymph node involvement.
keyPoints: Influenza-like illness with high fever is a common initial manifestation.
Incubation period: 3–5 days (range 1–21 days).
Lymph node enlargement depends on infection route.
Ulceroglandular form often presents with red, tender swollen lymph nodes.
Respiratory/pneumonic form may be bacteremic and severe, especially in older adults or those with comorbidities.

items.title: Diagnostic Challenges and Laboratory Findings in Tularemia
items.content: Routine biochemical tests are generally unhelpful for tularemia diagnosis due to nonspecific findings. However, C-reactive protein levels can be elevated, peaking around days 7–9 after onset.
keyPoints: Biochemical testing often shows unspecific results like elevated liver enzymes or hematuria.
In type B tularemia, white blood cell counts are normal in 70% of cases.
C-reactive protein levels peak at 78 mg/L (range 30–130) in outpatients.
Severe respiratory form shows CRP levels 100–400 mg/L with bacteremia and 50–220 mg/L without.

items.title: Optimal Specimen Collection for Tularemia Diagnosis
items.content: For accurate diagnosis of tularemia, specific clinical specimens should be collected. Ulcer specimens are best obtained from the edge of the wound using a slightly stiffer swab with some tissue rubbing to make it blood-tinged. If a scab is present, it must be removed before sampling.
keyPoints: Collect ulcer specimens from the edge of the wound.
Use a slightly stiffer swab and rub tissue to make it blood-tinged.
Remove any scabs before sampling.

items.title: Importance of Nucleic Acid Detection in Tularemia Diagnosis
items.content: Direct detection of F. tularensis-specific nucleic acid using PCR or similar methods is rapid and reliable, comparable to culture for ulcer specimens. This method can be applied directly from clinical specimens without prior culture, aiding in the identification of different F. tularensis varieties.
keyPoints: PCR and other nucleic acid detection methods are rapid and reliable.
Comparable sensitivity and specificity to culture for ulcer specimens.
Can identify different F. tularensis varieties directly from clinical specimens.

items.title: Serology as a Diagnostic Tool in Tularemia
items.content: Serology is essential for confirming tularemia, especially when suspected at later stages. Antibody responses are generally detectable 10–20 days post-infection and can be detected as early as 1–2 weeks after disease onset. A significant increase in antibody titers between two samples taken 2–4 weeks apart is crucial for a reliable acute-phase diagnosis.
keyPoints: Serology confirms tularemia, especially at later stages.
Antibody responses detectable 10–20 days post-infection.
Significant increase in antibody titers between two samples taken 2–4 weeks apart is necessary for acute-phase diagnosis.

items.title: Pathological Progression of Tularemia Skin Lesions
items.content: In tularemia, skin lesions initially show an accumulation of lymphocytes, plasma cells, and neutrophils at the infection site within the first week. Microabscesses with neutrophils form in the subcutis along with histiocytes and Langerhans cells in dilated lymph vessels. Skin ulceration and granuloma formation occur only after the first week, accompanied by lymph node engagement.
keyPoints: Initial lesion pathology includes lymphocytes, plasma cells, neutrophils
Microabscesses with neutrophils form in subcutis within the first week
Granulomas and skin ulcers develop after the first week

items.title: Antimicrobial Treatment Recommendations for Tularemia
items.content: Specific antimicrobial treatment is crucial to prevent complications in tularemia. Recommended antibiotics include ciprofloxacin, levofloxacin, gentamicin, and doxycycline. Macrolides like erythromycin and azithromycin are generally not recommended due to resistance issues with type B strains outside the U.S., though azithromycin may be considered for verified type A infections as per recent CDC guidelines.
keyPoints: Antibiotics of choice: ciprofloxacin, levofloxacin, gentamicin, doxycycline
Macrolides generally not recommended due to resistance in type B strains
Azithromycin may be used for verified type A infections

items.title: Lymph Node Abscess Formation and Antibiotic Treatment Timing in Tularemia
items.content: In tularemia, inflamed lymph nodes can progress to abscesses in about one-third of patients, particularly if there is a delay in initiating $F.\ tularensis$-specific antibiotic treatment beyond 2–3 weeks after disease onset. Even with appropriate antibiotics started late, abscess formation may occur. Culture specimens from such abscesses are typically negative for $F.\ tularensis$, but an additional 10-day course of antibiotics is often used to prevent further progression. In cases where there's a risk of spontaneous rupture, surgical intervention like drainage or aspiration is recommended.
keyPoints: Approximately one-third of patients may develop lymph node abscesses if antibiotic treatment is delayed beyond 2–3 weeks.
Culture specimens from abscesses are typically $F.\ tularensis$ negative.
An additional 10-day course of antibiotics is often used to prevent further progression.
Surgical drainage or aspiration is recommended if there's a risk of spontaneous rupture.

items.title: Impact of Antibiotic Treatment on Survival in Tularemia
items.content: The prognosis for tularemia has significantly improved with the use of antibiotics. Before antibiotics, case fatality rates were 5%–15%, rising to 60% for respiratory/pneumonic forms. Recent data from 2006-2021 showed a mortality rate of 3.0% among 903 patients. The presence of $F.\ tularensis$ in cultures was associated with reduced survival odds (OR, 0.1; 95% CI, 0.04–0.4). Treatment with high-efficacy antibiotics like aminoglycosides, fluoroquinolones, or tetracyclines significantly increased survival odds (adjusted OR, 10.4; 95% CI, 4.4–24.5), highlighting the importance of timely and appropriate antimicrobial therapy.
keyPoints: Case fatality rates were historically 5%–15%, rising to 60% for respiratory forms before antibiotics.
Recent mortality rate is 3.0% among 903 patients from 2006-2021.
Presence of $F.\ tularensis$ in cultures associated with reduced survival odds (OR, 0.1; 95% CI, 0.04–0.4).
High-efficacy antibiotics significantly increase survival odds (adjusted OR, 10.4; 95% CI, 4.4–24.5).

items.question: Which bacterium is responsible for causing tularemia?
items.explanation: Tularemia is caused by Francisella tularensis.

items.question: Which subspecies of F. tularensis has human disease documentation exclusively from the North American continent?
items.explanation: F. tularensis subspecies tularensis has human disease documentation exclusively from North America.

items.question: What is the biosafety level required for culturing F. tularensis in a laboratory?
items.explanation: Laboratory work with F. tularensis must be performed under biosafety level 3 conditions.

items.question: Which of the following routes is NOT commonly associated with human infection by F. tularensis?
options: Bites from mosquitoes, ticks, and tabanid flies
items.explanation: The passage does not mention consumption of undercooked meat as a route for F. tularensis infection.

items.question: In which region is tularemia primarily associated with mosquito bites?
options: Northern Boreal Forest regions of Sweden, Finland, and Russia
items.explanation: The passage states that in the Northern Boreal Forest regions of Sweden, Finland, and Russia, tularemia is primarily associated with mosquito bites.

items.question: What has been a significant factor contributing to the seasonal pattern of tularemia cases in the United States?
options: Increased human exposure to blood-feeding arthropods
items.explanation: The passage mentions that most cases occur in the summer, likely reflecting increased human exposure to blood-feeding arthropods.

items.question: Which of the following statements about F. tularensis subspecies is correct according to historic field studies?
options: Type A is associated with semiaquatic animals like muskrats and beavers
Type B is linked to dry environments with rabbits, ticks, domestic cats, and sheep
Type A is associated with rabbits, ticks, domestic cats, and sheep in dry environments
Both types are primarily maintained by protozoa such as amoeba
items.explanation: The passage states that type A tularemia is associated with rabbits, ticks, domestic cats, and sheep in comparatively dry environments.

items.question: Which tick life stage is most effective in transmitting F. tularensis to humans?
options: Nymphs, due to their high survival and transmission efficiency after ingestion of the bacteria.
Adult ticks, as they show rapid acquisition from bacteremic mice and high transmission efficiency.
Both nymphs and adult ticks are equally effective in transmitting F. tularensis.
Ticks do not play a significant role in transmitting F. tularensis to humans.
items.explanation: Adult ticks show rapid acquisition from bacteremic mice and high transmission efficiency (58–89%) compared to nymphs, which have low survival and transmission efficiency (0–13.5%).

items.question: What is the role of ticks in maintaining F. tularensis between outbreaks?
options: Ticks are obligate for maintaining F. tularensis between outbreaks.
Ticks are not considered obligate for maintaining F. tularensis between outbreaks.
Ticks have no role in maintaining F. tularensis between outbreaks.
Ticks maintain F. tularensis only during epizootics.
items.explanation: The literature is conflicted on the presence of transovarial transmission, suggesting ticks may not be obligate for maintaining F. tularensis between outbreaks.

items.question: How does F. tularensis typically enter the human body?
options: Through ingestion of contaminated food or water.
By inhalation of aerosols containing the bacteria.
Through microtrauma of the skin by an extremely low dose of bacteria.
items.explanation: Natural transmission to humans usually occurs through the skin or by inhalation, but ingestion is also possible; however, a very low dose (10–50 bacteria) suffices for infection via microtrauma of the skin or inhalation.

items.question: What immune response mechanisms are involved in combating F. tularensis after primary infection?
options: Complement, antibodies, neutrophils, inducible NO synthase, phagocyte oxidase, and cytokines such as interferon γ, tumor necrosis factor α, and interleukin 12.
B cells alone are sufficient to combat the infection.
Only cell-mediated responses involving T cells.
items.explanation: Effective early immune responses involve complement, antibodies, neutrophils, inducible NO synthase, phagocyte oxidase, and cytokines such as interferon γ, tumor necrosis factor α, and interleukin 12.

items.question: Which condition is NOT commonly misdiagnosed as tularemia due to its similar presentation?
items.explanation: The common cold is not typically misdiagnosed as tularemia, unlike lung cancer, head and neck cancer, or tuberculosis.

items.question: What should a tularemia-focused disease history include?
options: History of recent travel to tropical regions
Exposure risk factors for F. tularensis
items.explanation: A tularemia-focused disease history should include exposure risk factors for F. tularensis.

items.question: What is a critical aspect of managing patients with suspected tularemia?
options: High index of suspicion and awareness
items.explanation: A high index of suspicion and awareness is critical in managing patients with suspected tularemia.

items.question: Which lymphoid tissue is typically engaged when F. tularensis bacteria are inhaled?
options: Regional lymph nodes near the skin or oral mucous membranes
Lymph node enlargement in the mediastinum
Swollen lymph nodes in the inguinal region
items.explanation: Inhalation of F. tularensis results in lymph node enlargement in the mediastinum.

items.question: What is a common initial manifestation of tularemia?
items.explanation: The most common initial manifestation is an influenza-like illness with high fever.

items.question: Which form of tularemia may progress to severe disease requiring inpatient care, especially in older patients or those with comorbidities?
items.explanation: The respiratory or pneumonic form of tularemia may progress to severe disease requiring inpatient care.

items.question: Which laboratory method provides a definitive diagnosis of F. tularensis by allowing subsequent genetic methods to identify different varieties?
items.explanation: Culture identification provides a definitive diagnosis and allows for subsequent genetic methods to identify different F. tularensis varieties.

items.question: What is the recommended incubation period for blood cultures in order to achieve optimal sensitivity when diagnosing tularemia?
items.explanation: An incubation period of 10 days is recommended for optimal sensitivity in blood-culture diagnostics.

items.question: Which clinical specimen should be sampled from the edge of the wound and preferably made slightly blood-tinged when testing for F. tularensis?
items.explanation: Sampling from ulcers should be performed from the edge of the wound, preferably selecting a swab with a slightly stiffer stick and some rubbing of the tissue so that it becomes somewhat blood-tinged.

items.question: What is the mainstay diagnostic method for tularemia in many clinical microbiology laboratories?
items.explanation: Serology remains commonly used to confirm tularemia and is the mainstay diagnostic in many clinical microbiology laboratories.

items.question: Which histopathological finding is characteristic of tularemia skin lesions during the first week after infection?
options: Formation of granulomas and lymph node engagement
Accumulation of lymphocytes, plasma cells, and neutrophils at the infection site
Necrotizing pneumonia with abundant fibrin and cellular debris
Irregular microabscesses in liver and spleen
items.explanation: During the first week after F. tularensis skin infection, there is an accumulation of lymphocytes, plasma cells, and neutrophils at the site.

items.question: Which antibiotic class is generally not recommended for treating tularemia due to resistance in type B strains?
items.explanation: Macrolide antibiotics are not recommended for tularemia due to resistance in type B strains, particularly outside the United States.

items.question: Which antibiotic has been traditionally used as a first-line treatment for tularemia but is now less preferred compared to gentamicin?
items.explanation: Streptomycin was traditionally used for tularemia but is now less preferred due to drawbacks such as intramuscular administration and higher toxicity compared to gentamicin.

items.question: Which complication is associated with a delay of more than 2–3 weeks in initiating $ F. tularensis $-specific antibiotic treatment?
items.explanation: A delay in initiating specific antibiotic treatment is statistically associated with lymph node abscess formation.

items.question: What was the case fatality rate for respiratory/pneumonic tularemia prior to the use of antibiotics?
items.explanation: Prior to antibiotic treatment, the case fatality rate for respiratory/pneumonic tularemia could be as high as 60%.

items.question: Which antimicrobial drug class is associated with increased odds of survival when treating tularemia?
items.explanation: Treatment with a high-efficacy antimicrobial drug class such as aminoglycoside is associated with increased odds of survival.

items.statement: Francisella novicida is the causative agent of tularemia.
items.explanation: The causative agent of tularemia is Francisella tularensis, not Francisella novicida.

items.statement: Type A strains of F. tularensis are more lethal in experimentally infected rabbits than type B strains.
items.explanation: Type A strains are significantly more lethal in experimentally infected rabbits compared to type B strains.

items.statement: F. tularensis can infect a wide range of eukaryotic hosts including amphibians, birds, rodents, and ruminant mammals.
items.explanation: The passage confirms that F. tularensis is a generalist microbe capable of infecting many animals.

items.statement: Most human cases of tularemia in the United States occur during winter months.
items.explanation: The passage states that most cases in the U.S. occur during summer, not winter.

items.statement: F. tularensis can be transmitted to humans through ingestion of a very low dose of bacteria.
items.explanation: A large infection dose in the range of $10^{6}$–$10^{8}$ bacteria is required for oral transmission.

items.statement: F. tularensis escapes from phagosomes into the cytosol of host cells.
items.explanation: After uptake by tissue-resident macrophages and other local phagocytic cells, F. tularensis escapes from the phagosome into the cell cytosol.

items.statement: Tularemia can be easily diagnosed without considering exposure risk factors.
items.explanation: Considering exposure risk factors is essential for diagnosing tularemia.

items.statement: Physical examination in tularemia should assess the extent of inflammation at various anatomic locations.
items.explanation: Assessing the extent and location of inflammation is part of the physical examination for tularemia.

items.statement: Routine biochemical testing is highly specific and helpful for diagnosing tularemia.
items.explanation: Routine biochemical testing of blood generally is not helpful in tularemia because findings are unspecific.

items.statement: The incubation period for tularemia can range from 1 to 21 days.
items.explanation: The incubation period is usually 3–5 days but may range from 1 to 21 days.

items.statement: F. tularensis can be directly detected from clinical specimens using nucleic acid-based subtyping without prior culture.
items.explanation: With increased availability of molecular methods, nucleic acid-based subtyping without a prior culture step can be performed directly from clinical specimens.

items.statement: Serology is useful for diagnosing tularemia in the acute phase by detecting single titers of antibodies.
items.explanation: A reliable serologic diagnosis in the acute phase should rely on demonstrating a significant increase of antibody titers between two samples taken 2–4 weeks apart.

items.statement: Macrolide antibiotics are recommended for treating tularemia in the United States.
items.explanation: Azithromycin, a macrolide antibiotic, may be used as an alternative treatment choice for type A tularemia infections according to recent CDC guidelines.

items.statement: All beta-lactam drugs are effective against F. tularensis.
items.explanation: Beta-lactam drugs, including all classes, lack efficacy against F. tularensis.

items.statement: Culture specimens from an abscess at the disease stage are typically $ F. tularensis $ positive.
items.explanation: Culture specimens from an abscess at this stage of the disease are typically $ F. tularensis $ negative.

items.statement: The use of high-efficacy antimicrobial drugs has no impact on the survival odds for tularemia.
items.explanation: Treatment with at least one high-efficacy antimicrobial drug class is independently associated with increased odds for survival.

items.assertion: F. tularensis is maintained long-term in smaller geographic areas with repeated outbreaks.
items.reason: The disease has a patchy and geographically uneven distribution.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason explains why F. tularensis has a patchy distribution.

items.assertion: In Turkey, most cases of tularemia occur in winter due to contaminated drinking water.
items.reason: Colder temperatures enhance the survival of F. tularensis in water during winter months.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason explains why most cases occur in winter.

items.assertion: F. tularensis is considered a 'stealth pathogen'.
items.reason: The bacterium manipulates host-cell signaling to create a safe environment for replication inside the host cell.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why F. tularensis is considered a 'stealth pathogen'.

items.assertion: Necrotic lesions in nonhuman primates infected by inhalation are observed at day 4 after infection.
items.reason: These lesions likely mirror disease progression seen in severe human tularemia cases.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why these lesions are significant for understanding disease progression.

items.assertion: Patients with tularemia often present with symptoms similar to influenza.
items.reason: The acute illness in tularemia can be described as 'a severe virus' with high fever, muscle pain, and headache.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why patients with tularemia present similarly to influenza.

items.assertion: Chest imaging is useful for diagnosing respiratory tularemia.
items.reason: Single or multifocal consolidation may be present, accompanied by pleural effusion with hilar and mediastinal lymphadenopathy.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why chest imaging is useful in respiratory tularemia.

items.assertion: F. tularensis can be identified using MALDI-TOF mass spectrometry.
items.reason: Unexpected findings in blood cultures with MALDI-TOF identification have become more common due to improved blood culture systems and reference libraries.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why MALDI-TOF mass spectrometry is increasingly used for identifying F. tularensis.
